description: Band-level FAQ for Burnthday: the questions a new Widespread Panic fan actually asks, answered in the voice of a Spreadhead who has been there. Sourced where the fact came from the mined interviews/archive or from official/Relix/JamBase reporting; entries with "verify": true are general knowledge to confirm before publishing (none currently held). Grouped into sections and structured for a FAQ page + schema.org FAQPage JSON-LD.

faqs.question: Who's in the band?
faqs.answer: The lineup: John “JB” Bell (lead vocals, guitar), Dave Schools (bass), John “JoJo” Hermann (keys, since 1992), Domingo “Sunny” Ortiz (percussion, since the mid-'80s), Jimmy Herring (lead guitar, since 2006), and Duane Trucks (drums, since 2016). Founders no longer with us: Michael Houser (guitar), who died in 2002, and Todd Nance (drums), who left in 2016 and passed in 2020. George McConnell held the guitar chair in between (2002 to 2006). As of the 2026 tour, Nick Johnson is playing lead while Jimmy steps away for cancer treatment. The band says Jimmy's prognosis is positive and he is expected back.

sources.label: An Update on Jimmy (official, 1/8/2026)
Relix: Herring to step away temporarily, Nick Johnson to fill in

faqs.question: Where does the name "Widespread Panic" come from?
faqs.answer: From guitarist Michael Houser's actual panic attacks. Friends had taken to calling him “Panic,” and while he was toying with names like “Widespread Depression Orchestra,” the two collided into Widespread Panic. Houser said the attacks stopped after he met his wife.

sources.label: Michael Houser interview (conducted 8/22/1997), Relix Oct/Nov 2003

faqs.question: How did Sunny Ortiz join the band?
faqs.answer: He rolled into Athens from Texas, wandered into the Uptown Lounge with his bongos still out in the car, and the guys asked him to sit in. He figured they were punks. He wasn't wrong, but he liked it, and he's been the percussion engine ever since (mid-'80s).

sources.label: Michael Houser interview (8/22/1997), Relix Oct/Nov 2003

faqs.question: How did JoJo join the band?
faqs.answer: Good circumstances for us, tougher ones for the guy he replaced. Panic actually had a keyboard player before JoJo. T Lavitz of the Dixie Dregs was brought in at Capricorn's urging to fill out the sound on the 1991 self-titled record and the tour behind it, but the band's brutal schedule wore on him and he stepped off early in 1992. In came John “JoJo” Hermann, an old friend whose Oxford, Mississippi band Beanland was winding down. His tryout was an acoustic set at the Georgia Theatre on March 4, 1992, the band opening for Tower of Power and hiding the set behind a joke name. He passed, played his first proper show a week and a half later, and he's been our piano man ever since.

sources.label: JamBase: JoJo Hermann's Widespread Panic debut (1992)
JamBase: Happy Birthday John “JoJo” Hermann — Making His Panic Debut, 1992

faqs.question: Do they write their own songs, and whose songs do they cover?
faqs.answer: Mostly originals, and to this day they credit them collectively to “Widespread Panic” rather than to any one member. It never stopped: the 2024 record Hailbound Queen still opens its liner notes with “All songs written by Widespread Panic,” then spells out the exceptions one by one. Their covers lean on their Athens and Southern circle: Bloodkin (Danny Hutchens), the late Vic Chesnutt, and Jerry Joseph, plus deep blues and funk like Junior Kimbrough and the Meters. The whole band, not just JB, moonlighted as Brute, a long-running collaboration with Vic Chesnutt that put out two albums, Nine High a Pallet (1995) and Co-Balt (2002). Schools has run with Hard Working Americans on the side.

sources.label: Everyday Companion; band interviews (see Song Origins)
Hailbound Queen liner notes (2024), official Bandcamp
Brute — Co-Balt (personnel: the full Panic lineup + Vic Chesnutt)

faqs.question: When and where did Widespread Panic start?
faqs.answer:
Fast version. JB and Michael Houser met at the University of Georgia in the early '80s and started as a guitar duo. By the mid-'80s they were a full band grinding it out in Athens, playing an A-frame house and dollar-Monday nights at the Uptown Lounge before Space Wrangler landed in 1988. Keys came in stages, T Lavitz of the Dixie Dregs first, then JoJo Hermann for good in 1992. That same year they helped launch H.O.R.D.E., the traveling festival they co-founded with Blues Traveler, Phish, the Spin Doctors, and Col. Bruce Hampton's Aquarium Rescue Unit, the tour that carried the whole jam scene out of the clubs.

The longer version fills in the grind. JB and Mikey met as University of Georgia freshmen in 1981, roomed together, and wrote songs that are still setlist staples, “Chilly Water” and “Driving Song” among them. They cut their teeth on the Athens circuit the hard way, fraternity parties and bars, night after night, before drummer Todd Nance, a childhood friend Houser called up, completed the lineup for the band's first shows as Widespread Panic in early 1986. A deal with Atlanta's Landslide Records followed in 1987, and Space Wrangler arrived the next year.

One more piece of that early crew is worth knowing. Garrie Vereen pulled his truck up to the band's house in 1986 to help haul gear and never really left, becoming their equipment manager and, in JB's words, the band's “ambassador to the rest of the music world.” To Spreadheads he's also the voice tied to the band's own Athens tagline, sending them out “all the way from Athens, Georgia.” He passed in 2011, and the band still honors him.

image.alt: Widespread Panic performing at the A-Frame house in Athens, Georgia, February 1985
caption: First Widespread Panic show at the band's A-Frame house in Athens, GA, 02/24/1985. Photo by Neal Becton.
02/24/85 · A-Frame, Weymanda Court, Athens, GA

sources.label: New Georgia Encyclopedia: Widespread Panic
Widespread Panic — Wikipedia (met 1981; Todd Nance, first show 1986; Landslide 1987)
Flagpole: Widespread Panic Celebrates 30 Years of Athens Music
Garrie Vereen 1961–2011 (official memorial; JB on Vereen as the band's ambassador)
H.O.R.D.E. tour — Wikipedia

faqs.question: And from the major-label years to now?
faqs.answer: They signed to Capricorn and cut the major-label records Everyday (1993) and Ain't Life Grand (1994), then toured without mercy. Early in 1996 they ran the Sit n Ski tour through Western ski towns, an acoustic-leaning run where the band played sitting down. Two years later came the Athens free show for Light Fuse Get Away, roughly 100,000 people downtown. The hardest turn was 2002, when Mikey died of cancer. George McConnell held the guitar chair from 2002 to 2006, and Jimmy Herring has had it since. In 2012 they hung up the electrics for the Wood Tour, their first all-acoustic run, and released the live album Wood. Running under all of it is an Allman Brothers thread, from Warren Haynes sit-ins going back to 1991 to the 2009 Southbound co-tour.

sources.label: Ain't Life Grand (1994), Capricorn/Warner — Wikipedia
JamBase: Sit N Ski With Widespread Panic (1996 acoustic tour)
Wood (live album, 2012) — Wikipedia
Jambands: Panic and the Allmans Head Southbound (2009)

faqs.question: Who was Michael Houser?
faqs.answer:
The founding guitarist and the sonic heart of the band. Everyone called him Mikey. He was quiet, unshowy, and completely singular, and the sound of Widespread Panic at its core is the sound of his playing.

That sound came from touch as much as from notes. Mikey layered reverb and delay and rode a volume pedal with his right foot, swelling each note in from silence so the pick attack disappeared, a rhythmic, wave-like wash that rolled under the band instead of sitting on top of it. It's why his leads feel less like solos and more like weather moving through.

He played nearly to the end, including the first Bonnaroo in 2002, before pancreatic cancer took him on August 10, 2002. If you want to know what this band sounds like at its heart, it's him.

pullquote.text: When I play a lead, it's describing something that I kind of see or hear in my head. A natural outpouring. That's why I love playing: there's nothing else that I can do better than I play lead guitar. We met, we all realized that we had the same vision. It's the most important thing that we do, a very healing sort of thing. Each one of us gets that something out of the show. That THING.

sources.label: Relix, Oct/Nov 2003, “Southeast of Eden”

faqs.question: Why did Mikey sit down?
faqs.answer: Two reasons, and neither is the one people assumed. Houser had actually sat to play early on (1986–87), but so many fans thought he was blind, one after another telling him “I'm sorry you can't see,” that he stood up just to stop the questions, even though sitting was always more comfortable. The bigger problem was physical: he worked a volume pedal constantly with his right foot, which left him balanced stock-still on his left leg for three hours a night and wrecked his leg and back. Eventually he decided, in his words, “I have to sit down, and if people don't like it I guess I'm gonna have to start driving a taxi or something.” He found he played better seated, with more range, and nobody ever gave him grief for it. Fans mark March 14, 1997 in Park City as the night he took his seat for good, after a “last stand” run of standing shows in Vail.

image.alt: Michael Houser of Widespread Panic playing guitar seated
caption: Michael Houser, seated, in his final years with the band.

sources.label: Michael Houser, “Detours” interview (shared by Burnthday)
Michael Houser interview (8/22/1997), Relix Oct/Nov 2003

faqs.question: Was Space Wrangler their first record?
faqs.answer:
It was their debut studio album (1988), but not their first release. That was a self-pressed 45 in 1986 on the band's own Space Baby label, only 500 copies, whose B-side “Monkey Image” is a shortened version of “Sleepy Monkey.” JB has told the story of driving from their Athens house to Atlanta to pick the box of singles up from the presser.

One for the collectors: Dave Schools has said the very first pressing of Space Wrangler had the labels put on the wrong sides, so genuine misprints are out there for anyone willing to dig through crates. The mismatch is documented too, the jacket and the label disagree about which song opens side one. The record came out on Atlanta's Landslide label in 1988, and the band has since reissued much of its back catalog on vinyl, Space Wrangler included, so you don't need the misprint to hear it.

sources.label: Everyday Companion, Song Histories (Sleepy Monkey)
Widespread Panic debut-single reissue
Space Wrangler — Wikipedia (original Landslide pressing; jacket/label side mismatch)
Back Catalog Vinyl Reissues (official, 2014)

faqs.question: What's the biggest show they ever played?
faqs.answer:
Panic in the Streets, April 18, 1998. To celebrate the release of their live album Light Fuse Get Away, the band threw a free show on Washington Street outside the 40 Watt Club in their hometown of Athens. They figured maybe 20,000 might turn up. Something close to 100,000 packed downtown, and it still stands as the largest record-release party ever thrown. For scale of a different kind, look at Bonnaroo: Panic was the very first band booked for the inaugural festival in 2002 and headlined both of its nights, one of six times they'd play the Manchester farm.

That first Bonnaroo produced one of the band's own favorite memories. They pulled gospel singer Dottie Peoples and her People's Choice Choir out for “Tall Boy,” and Dave Schools still talks about it: “I watched 80,000 music lovers literally levitate as Dottie channeled the spirit.” If you want to understand what this band means to Georgia, though, start with Athens.

links.label: Watch: “Tall Boy” with Dottie Peoples, Bonnaroo 2002

sources.label: Athens 1998 (official)
JamBase: Panic Performs Before Massive Crowd At Hometown Concert In 1998
Relix: Bonnaroo 2002 — Widespread Panic with Dottie Peoples “Tall Boy”
Jambands: My First Roo (Dave Schools on Bonnaroo 2002)

faqs.question: Is it true the drummer is an Allman Brother?
faqs.answer: Close. Duane Trucks is Trucks royalty. He's the nephew of Allman Brothers founding drummer Butch Trucks and the younger brother of guitarist Derek Trucks of Tedeschi Trucks Band. He started subbing for Todd Nance in 2014 and took the drum chair for good in 2016. And in a twist most casual fans miss, he married into another Panic family: Duane is guitarist Jimmy Herring's son-in-law.

sources.label: Duane Trucks — Wikipedia
ARTS ATL: Duane Trucks finds his groove with Widespread Panic

faqs.question: Are they still touring?
faqs.answer: Yes, very much so, and 2026 turned into one of the more storied stretches of the whole run. Jimmy Herring is off the road getting cancer treatment, so Nick Johnson is handling lead guitar (more on Nick below), and the band has carried it beautifully. Fans got the first taste when Nick and Billy Strings both sat in over the New Year's run at the Fox Theatre in Atlanta, before the 2026 tour got rolling. In June they ran their 78th straight sellout at Red Rocks with Nick slinging solos like he'd been there for years. We're all pulling for Jimmy and holding his seat.

sources.label: An Update on Jimmy (official, 1/8/2026)
Relix: Panic conclude New Year's run with Billy Strings
Westword: Panic's historic 2026 Red Rocks run

faqs.question: Who's Nick Johnson, the guitarist filling in for Jimmy?
faqs.answer: A Georgia guitar lifer, even if he'll tell you he wasn't a Spreadhead himself. There's a full-circle twist to him taking Jimmy's chair: Nick moved to Atlanta specifically to study under Jimmy Herring at the Atlanta Institute of Music, only for Jimmy to land the Panic gig right as Nick arrived, so the lessons never happened. By then Nick had already been playing with Duane Trucks and bassist Kevin Scott, and the three of them came up together under Col. Bruce Hampton, the Atlanta mad-scientist mentor whose orbit shaped half this story. Nick went on to spend 13 years in Randall Bramblett's band, about the strongest Georgia-soul pedigree you can carry. Duane called him on December 23 with the news that Jimmy wanted the 2026 dates honored but couldn't make them, and Nick got the nod. His first full run as our guy was Panic en la Playa 13 down in Mexico. The whole thing keeps it in the family: Bramblett and Sam Holt, Mikey's old guitar tech who leads Outformation, are exactly the kind of Athens-orbit players who have turned up on Panic stages for years.

sources.label: JamBase: Nick Johnson Talks Getting The Call To Tour With Widespread Panic
ARTS ATL: 30 Under 30 — guitarist Nick Johnson (Jimmy Herring, AIM, Kevin Scott, Duane Trucks, Col. Bruce)
ARTS ATL: From the Randall Bramblett Band to Widespread Panic
Flagpole: Sunny Ortiz and Sam Holt on Athens, Panic and Keeping the Music Alive

faqs.question: They're called a “jam band” — do they play the same set every night?
faqs.answer: No, and that's the whole point. They carry well over 100 songs, rewrite the setlist every night, try not to repeat a song within a run, and stretch tunes out with long improvisation. No two shows are the same. That's exactly why Spreadheads follow them city to city.

faqs.question: Who sits in with Panic?
faqs.answer:
Constantly, and that's the Athens way. Widespread Panic came up inside a whole web of Southern players, so their stage has always had a revolving door. In the early days it was fiddle: David Blackmon sawed away on Space Wrangler and turned up for years after, and the tradition carried through guests like Nicky Sanders of the Steep Canyon Rangers, the “Nicky Fiddle” you'll hear Spreadheads talk about.

The Allman-Brothers and Georgia-soul family runs deep. Derek Trucks, Randall Bramblett, and Chuck Leavell all guested on the Athens live album Live in the Classic City back in 2000, and all three keep coming back, Bramblett a regular for decades, Leavell as recently as the band's 75th straight Red Rocks sellout in 2025, where he sat in on Stones and Pink Floyd covers. More recently, bluegrass phenom Billy Strings joined the 2026 New Year's run at the Fox.

Then there are the marquee one-offs. Carlos Santana made his only appearance with the band in 2001 at Berkeley's Greek Theatre, tearing through Funkadelic. Two years before that, Bob Weir of the Grateful Dead climbed aboard at the 1999 Aspen Harmony Festival for a 25-minute “Pusherman.” Different worlds, same porch.

sources.label: Tomorrow's Verse: Fiddling Around With Widespread Panic (David Blackmon, Nicky Sanders)
JamBase: Carlos Santana & Widespread Panic, Berkeley Greek 2001
Live For Live Music: Bob Weir & RatDog Join Widespread Panic, Aspen 1999
Chuck Leavell: Widespread Panic plays Stones & Pink Floyd with Chuck Leavell, 75th Red Rocks sellout (2025)
Widespread Panic: Live in the Classic City (Derek Trucks, Randall Bramblett, Chuck Leavell, 2000)

faqs.question: Can I record their shows, and where can I hear live tapes?
faqs.answer: Yes, and it is one of the things that built this band. Widespread Panic has welcomed tapers from day one, and the trading community that grew up around those recordings carried the music to new fans years before the industry caught on. Asked in a 1995 online fan chat whether taping helps or hurts the band, Dave Schools did not hesitate: “I'm all for live audience taping!” Decades of shows stream free on archive.org and the Relisten app, and the taper section is still working every night.

sources.label: Shows on archive.org (Live Music Archive)
Dave Schools online fan chat (3/7/1995), transcript via Nothing But Widespread Panic

faqs.question: What are Widespread Panic's big traditions?
faqs.answer: Red Rocks is the big one. Their runs at the Colorado amphitheater are a Spreadhead pilgrimage, and they own the venue's record for consecutive sellouts, 78 straight after the June 2026 run and counting, a streak nobody sees getting broken. Beyond that: Halloween shows heavy on costumes and rare covers, the New Year's Eve run, and Panic en la Playa, their destination event on the beach in Mexico.

sources.label: Westword: Panic reach 78 straight Red Rocks sellouts (2026)

faqs.question: Which albums should a new fan start with?
faqs.answer: Start with the studio run: Space Wrangler (1988), Ain't Life Grand (1994), Bombs & Butterflies (1997), and 'Til the Medicine Takes (1999). Then, because Panic is above all a live band, go to Light Fuse Get Away (1998), the definitive live document. For where they are right now, they broke a nine-year studio silence in 2024 with a pair of companion records, Snake Oil King in June and Hailbound Queen that fall, their first new studio music since 2015. After that, quit reading and go pull a tape, because the real Panic is the one happening on stage tonight.

sources.label: JamBase: 'King' Gets A Partner — Widespread Panic Preps 2nd New Album Of 2024, Hailbound Queen (companion to Snake Oil King)
Hailbound Queen (2024), official Bandcamp
